data.title: NIO: Trade War Could Be A Major Boon
data.description:
NIO’s (NYSE:NIO) recent earnings were a mixed bag. The company reported over 40% YoY growth in deliveries and more than 400 basis point YoY improvement in gross margin. However, despite this improvement, the company reported an EPS and revenue miss. There has also been a dilution, as the company raised $450 million from a share placement. Since the recent earnings call, the stock has dropped over 30%, which shows the negative sentiment towards the stock. There are a few major tailwinds that should help NIO in the next few quarters. I believe that the recent tariff war between the U.S. and China could improve NIO’s position in the domestic and international markets. According to the CEO, NIO has 40% market share in China’s premium BEV market priced over RMB300,000 or over $41,000. Tesla (TSLA) could face consumer and regulatory backlash in China, which can help NIO in the premium segment. In the previous article, I mentioned that NIO could deliver good deliveries in the earnings call.

NIO is also planning on launching its Firefly model in the European Union soon. The European Commission had imposed tariffs on Chinese EVs last year, but the changing trading dynamics could lead to a better negotiated tariff settlement between China and the European Union. This could help improve NIO’s margin and profitability in the next few quarters. NIO’s losses are the main headwind for the stock sentiment, and any improvement in this metric could deliver a bullish momentum to the stock. The consensus revenue estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026 is $16.39 billion, which gives NIO stock a forward P/S ratio of 0.48, making it quite attractive.

Mixed earnings and price drop

NIO reported deliveries of 72,689 in Q4 2024 compared to 50,045 in Q4 2023. This is over 45% YoY growth, which is quite good when we compare with the 13% YoY decline reported by Tesla in the recent Q1 press release. NIO’s premium cars are competing directly with Tesla in China and giving it good competition. We have seen Tesla reduce prices a number of times in the last few quarters due to higher competition.

Figure: NIO’s deliveries in the last few quarters. Source: Company Filings

Despite the intense competition, NIO has been able to improve the margin metrics. The vehicle margin improved to 13.1% in Q4 2024 from 11.9% in Q4 2023. The gross margin also improved to 11.7% in Q4 2024 from 7.5% in Q4 2023. This is a 420 basis points improvement in one year, which is quite good when we look at the pricing wars within its segment.

Figure: Margin improvement by NIO. Source: Company Filings

At the same time, NIO missed its EPS by $0.11, showing negative EPS of $0.44. The revenue metric was also missed by $52 million.

Figure: EPS and revenue miss by NIO. Source: Seeking Alpha

This changed the sentiment towards the stock, leading to a 30% correction since the earnings. However, despite the EPS and revenue miss, the fundamentals look quite good. The faster delivery growth helps in gaining economies of scale for its battery swap technology. More people using this option will help in achieving faster break-even in this segment.

Trade war could be a major tailwind

Over the last few weeks, the tariff situation has gripped Wall Street. Analysts are still looking at the possible impact on different industries and segments. One likely scenario is that there are higher purchases of domestic brands in China. Tesla and Musk are close to the current White House administration, and we could see a customer backlash against this brand in China. Tesla’s recent earnings showed a revenue of $20.9 billion from China in 2024 compared to $21.7 billion in 2023. This slowdown in sales in China could accelerate significantly due to the tariff wars.

Figure: Tesla’s revenue from different regions. Source: Tesla Filings

NIO’s CEO mentioned that the company has a 40% market share in China’s BEV segment priced over RMB300,000 (approximately $41,000). There is a direct competition between Nio and Tesla in this segment. Any major decline in Tesla’s sales could reduce the pricing pressure from NIO and allow the company to show better deliveries and margins.

Figure: CEO’s comments in the recent earnings. Source: Company Filings

Another likely situation to consider is the changing dynamics between China and the European Union. Due to the higher tariffs from the US, China would need a better trading arrangement with the European Union in order to sell any excess goods. The European Union had earlier announced additional tariffs on Chinese EVs, going as high as 45% in October 2024. Recent announcements show a rapid move towards removing these tariffs and setting an alternative minimum price for cars.

NIO will soon launch its Firefly model in the EU. It faces an additional tariff of 20.7%, bringing the total tariffs to 30.7%. A better trading negotiation between China and the EU can bring down these tariffs and allow NIO to expand in a very important market. A successful launch in the EU could boost the margins for the company and change the forward revenue and EPS projections of the company.

Attractive price after recent dip

According to consensus estimates, NIO is expected to report 38.2% YoY revenue growth in the fiscal year ending Dec 2025 and another 30.6% YoY revenue growth in the fiscal year ending Dec 2026. This is a very strong revenue growth momentum. At the same time, the company has shown a good improvement in gross margin, which should help improve the overall profitability. The stock is currently trading at a P/S ratio of 0.68 for the fiscal year ending Dec 2025 and a 0.48 P/S ratio for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026. This is quite attractive pricing for an EV stock, which has a number of positive tailwinds working in its favor.

Figure: Forward revenue estimates of NIO. Source: Seeking Alpha

A big factor in the future stock momentum is the ability of the management to deliver on its promised cost cuts and operating efficiency. NIO’s gross margin is still behind market leader, BYD. As mentioned above, if we see a lower competitive environment in the premium BEV segment in China, it could help NIO improve its margins. Entry into lucrative international markets like the EU should also boost the margin growth potential.

Despite some negative sentiments towards NIO, I believe the company is in a good position due to changing trading dynamics and a better competitive environment in China. Higher margins and a decline in losses could lead to a rapid improvement in market sentiment and deliver good returns in the stock, making it a Buy at the current price.

Investor Takeaway

NIO stock has seen a 30% dip after the recent earnings. The company missed both the EPS and revenue estimates. However, it was able to report over 45% YoY growth in deliveries and a 420 basis points YoY improvement in gross margin. NIO has over 40% market share in the premium BEV segment in China, where it competes with Tesla. We could see a greater customer preference towards domestic brands due to the current geopolitical tensions, which can lower the competitive pressure on NIO and improve its margins.

A launch in the lucrative EU market will also be a tailwind for the stock. We could see a better trading arrangement between China and the EU with lower tariffs, as both deal with higher tariffs from the US. Lower tariffs could improve NIO’s performance in the EU and boost the margins rapidly.

data.title: Upslope Capital's Q1 2025 Investor Letter
data.description:
Dear Fellow Investor,

Upslope’s objective is to deliver attractive, equity-like returns with significantly reduced market risk and low correlation versus traditional equity strategies. Q1 was challenging. However, in the two weeks since Mar 31, the world has changed, owing to an intense realignment of global trade policies. This has swiftly aligned markets more with Upslope’s portfolio and approach. April-to-date performance1 has been strong (approx. +7%), leaving the Fund +2% YTD (vs. -12% for the S&P Midcap 400).

Upslope Exposure & Returns2

Benchmark Returns

Average Net Long3

Net Return

S&P Midcap 400 ETF (MDY)

HFRX Equity Hedge Index

Q1 2025

33%

-5.1%

-6.1%

+0.2%

Last 12 Months

34%

-1.6%

-2.9%

+4.5%

Since Inception

34%

+9.1%

+8.9%

+4.6%

Downside Deviation

5.7%

13.1%

4.7%

Sortino Ratio4

1.26

0.53

0.56

Note: LPs/clients should always check individual statements for returns, which may vary due to timing, fee schedules and other factors. Since inception returns, downside standard deviation, and Sortino are all annualized figures.

MARKET CONDITIONS – A CRAZY GAME OF POKER

I don’t know how the global trade war will shake out, but I do think that recent events will: (1) dampen valuation multiples in the U.S. (already high on an absolute basis and relative to the rest of the world) and

(2) put the outlook for earnings at great risk. Why?

  • Investors around the world have discovered that a small group of individuals in the U.S. has the ability to rapidly implement major policy changes that materially impact businesses, trading partners, and allies. Regardless of whether one views these changes as good or bad, this means less predictability and higher volatility – factors that necessarily lead to lower valuation multiples.
  • Consumer and corporate confidence have taken an enormous hit year-to-date. “Weaker than during the 2008 Financial Crisis,” as Apollo’s Torsten Sløk recently put it. Why confidence is down isn’t very important. What matters is that without a rapid U-turn, it bodes poorly for earnings.

Given the above, Upslope’s portfolio continues to skew towards overseas investments, which should benefit from better valuations, a continued reversal of 15+ years of investor flows, and, an improving fiscal outlook. While Q1 had already been busy in terms of portfolio adjustments, activity accelerated in April as the crisis unfolded. This is logical and similar to past crises: when equities move as much in a single day as they often do in a full year, opportunities and mistakes bubble up much faster, and action is warranted.

PORTFOLIO POSITIONING

As of this writing, gross and beta-adjusted net exposures were 146% and 49%, respectively. Positioning reflects an above average number of perceived opportunities on the long side of the portfolio.

PORTFOLIO UPDATES

The largest contributors to and detractors from quarterly performance are noted below. Gross contribution to overall portfolio return is noted in parentheses.

Key Longs Exited

AptarGroup (ATR, specialty pharmaceutical and food/bev packaging business) – the Fund exited Aptar after owning shares nearly since inception. This wasn’t a hard thesis break, but I sold due to continued management malaise and significantly better opportunities elsewhere due to recent market dislocations.

Charles River (CRL, testing, discovery, and safety for biopharma) – sold due to a thesis break when the FDA announced the phase-out of animal testing requirements for certain drugs. This was a risk I’d worried about, and given an already-challenging environment for the company I decided the thesis (cyclical bottom) had been broken.

VF Corp (VFC, parent company of North Face, Vans, and other brands) – I began selling VF in Q1 because as markets corrected it became clear that other more straight-forward investment opportunities were emerging (vs. VF’s somewhat high-wire turnaround). The announcement of potentially aggressive tariffs in early April solidified this view.

Barry Callebaut (BARN-SW (OTCPK:BYCBF)(OTCPK:BRRLY), leading outsourced chocolate producer) – finished exiting Barry Callebaut early in Q1 on a thesis (timing cocoa peak) break. The Fund continues to own shares of Hershey, as the durability of its brands should give it greater staying power.

Current Long Portfolio Discussion

Given how fluid markets are, a discussion of the Fund’s overall long portfolio and its construction seems like a better use of time and space than a detailed review of individual holdings. I plan to elaborate further on some of the Fund’s new positions in upcoming quarterly letters and/or monthly Fact Sheets (note: the latter is distributed to current LPs and prospective investors – contact me if you would like to receive these and are not currently). For now, a brief summary of a few new positions is included below.

Upslope’s long portfolio can be divided into four distinct categories. This bucketing is partly deliberate (top-down search) and partly by accident (landed on similar bottom-up opportunities). Sizing is roughly equal among the categories, which are (details on each further below):

  • Re-arm and Re-shore
  • European Resurgence
  • Long Vol(atility)
  • Out-of-Favor Defensives Re-arm and Re-shore

This has been an unofficial, if deliberate, theme in the portfolio since early 2022, around the outset of the war in Ukraine. The war, in conjunction with the pandemic, I believe created lasting global change in terms of how countries view defense spending and supply chains. The Fund’s disclosed holdings include: Teledyne (TDY, specialty sensors and electronics for aerospace/defense and industrials – effectively spanning “re-arm and re-shore”), as well as the Fund’s European defense investments (Chemring (OTCPK:CMGMF)(OTCPK:CMGMY) and QinetiQ (OTCPK:QNTQF)(QNTQY)). During Q1 and in April, the Fund also added two Starter positions that should benefit from long-term reshoring.

European Resurgence

I’ve discussed the Fund’s outsized European exposure, which came about organically, for some time now. I effectively consider developments YTD – most notably German fiscal reform and the breakout of a global trade war – as confirmation of the “overweight” Europe thesis. In light of these changes, as well as attractive relative valuations and underweight investor positioning in Europe, I continued to add to the Fund’s European long exposure in Q1. In addition to the defense longs, the Fund also owns several ‘quality’ industrials of varying cyclicality:

  • Diploma (DPLM-LON)(OTCPK:DPMAY)(OTC:DPLMF) – specialty distributor focused on essential consumables across life sciences, seals (machinery), and controls (wiring and other) – owned since 2020.
  • Spirax (SPX-LON)(OTCPK:SPXSF)(OTCPK:SPXSY) – provider of steam systems, pumps, and electric thermal solutions for industrial and biopharma applications.
  • Holcim (HOLN-SWX)(OTCPK:HCMLF)(OTCPK:HCMLY) – global aggregates, cement and building materials supplier with deep expertise in emerging markets and a pending spinoff of US operations.
  • Sandvik (SAND-SE)(OTCPK:SDVKF)(OTCPK:SDVKY) – Sweden-based provider of mining, metal-cutting, and other industrial equipment and tools with outsized exposure to precious metals miners.

Each of the above companies has at least one of the following attributes that I believe will help partially blunt the impact of a potential trade war: essential products at a low cost, monopoly-like offerings, localized production/sales, and superior technology.

Long Vol(atility)

Given the geopolitical climate, I believe volatility will be elevated for several years (although clearly not at the current extreme level). Given this, volume-driven financial exchanges and other businesses that benefit from macro volatility should continue to thrive. The Fund’s disclosed holdings include: CME Group (CME) and Japan Exchange (OTCPK:JPXGY)(OTCPK:OSCUF).

In Q1, the Fund also added MarketAxess (MKTX, a leading credit and fixed income exchange), a business Upslope has owned (and at times shorted) several times over the years. MKTX has had a challenging five years or so, but (1) should see stabilized/improved market share with higher volatility, (2) trades at a deep enough discount to consider the possibility of a take-out in the near-to-medium term.

The Fund also added a Starter in a business services company that benefits from elevated macro volatility.

Out-of-Favor Defensives

Typically, the rising prospect of a recession leads to sharp outperformance of defensively cyclical businesses (e.g. consumer staples). And while that has indeed been the case, for a variety of reasons the market has also seen dispersion in the group, with a larger than typical (from my observation) number left behind. The Fund’s disclosed holdings include: Hershey and DSM Firmenich. The Fund also added three Starters, including shares of a company I’ve followed for a decade trading near a decade-low valuation, despite strong execution. The other two are healthcare related.

CLOSING THOUGHTS

Much has changed since the last letter: a lot of exuberance has receded, defensives have begun to outperform, and international (ex-US) equities have come into relative favor. Given how long defensives and international stocks were out of favor – the latter backed by 15+ years of investor flows – it’s hard to think these trends will revert back soon, even if a near-term pause might be due.

As always, thank you for the trust you’ve placed in me and Upslope to manage a portion of your hard- earned money. Please contact me if you have any questions at all, would like to add to your investment, or know a qualified investor who may be a good fit for Upslope’s unique approach.

Sincerely,

George K. Livadas

1-720-465-7033 | george@upslopecapital.com

Footnotes

1 Estimates are net of fees for Class A interest and as of April 11, 2025 close.

2 Returns prior to June 2023 are for a composite of all separate accounts invested according to Upslope’s long/short strategy. Subsequent returns are for Class A interest in Upslope Partners Fund, LP. See important performance-related details and disclosures in Appendix A.

3 Beta-/delta-adjusted.

4 Calculated as: (Net return since inception - 2.0% risk-free rate) / downside deviation.

5 Previously a “Starter” position that has now been exited.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tan Brings The Knife, Intel Stock Will Bring The Gains: Reiterating Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Last time I covered Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC), my rating was a buy based on their new CEO Li-Bu Tan being a lot tougher on trimming the fat. I previously stated that Tan would make significant changes at Intel, and it seems that's precisely what he has done. On Monday, Intel announced it would be selling half its stake in Altera to Silver Lake, a move that frees up quite a bit of capital for Intel and also offloads a significant weight on their shoulders in terms of workforce.

I once said that Intel wasn’t a stock for the long game, and I still remain true to that. The stock is down 21% this past month among all the news about tariffs, and jumping in at current levels around $20 per share or below could prove the short-term turnaround stock investors are hoping for.

I believe that Tan isn't finished yet in terms of efficiency changes, with further job cuts still to come. Intel is expected to report earnings very soon, in just over a week (April 24th), with the changes Tan has already made, I expect Intel to beat earnings estimates of $12.31 billion in next quarter earnings, which is already on the lower end of previous expectations. I’m still buy-rated on Intel, and current selloffs due to newly announced tariffs only open up more entry points for investors looking for a near-term gain.

Intel Trimming the Fat

Intel stock is currently trading around $20.30, up 3.4% since Monday on the news of the Altera sale. For those investors that don't know, Altera was a daughter organization of Intel focused on producing programmable chips. Intel is selling a majority stake in the company, so 51% will go to Silver Lake for a price tag of $4.5 billion. This values Altera at $8.75 billion.

If you ask me, this is a huge weight off Intel's shoulders and a wise move by Tan. Since Intel acquired Altera in 2015, it has significantly underperformed, only contributing to 3% of Intel’s revenue in 2024 and posting an operating loss of $615 million. On top of this, Altera has roughly 3,000 employees, so Intel offloading over half of those paychecks trims the fat further.

Tan has always remained focused on shifting Intel back to what it’s good. Competitors like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) are tipped to be making a deal with TSMC (TSM) which will allow them to manufacture chips at their foundry in Arizona, giving them some reprise from any upcoming tariffs. I expect the cash from Intel's latest deal with Silver Lake to give them so breathing room, especially to help reverse their steep operating loss of over $13 billion reported in 2024. I’m expecting Tan to experiment with more cost-cutting measures, and think this pocketed $4.5 billion could better position him to do just that. Year-to-date intel is up 1.24%, which is a much more attractive number than the S&P 500's (SP500) 7.74% decline over the same period.

Intel is expected to report earnings on the 24th of this month. Wall Street puts guidance at $12.31 billion, which is in line with Intel's guidance back in January of $11.7-12.7 billion.

So, can investors expect Tan's cost-cutting efficiency to show up in this upcoming report earnings? I believe they can, and here’s why. Within the current 90-day tariff pause, I expect the trading war to end, and Intel will be totally tariff-free. To back this up, news came over the weekend that semiconductors would be free from reciprocal tariffs. This foreshadows the long-term future for chip tariffs, and their effects should be negligible on Intel. Along with this, Microsoft Windows 10 support is expected to end in October, and pre-orders for PCs should show up in the next report earning guidance; this makes me believe that Intel should guide much higher for Q3, reflecting increased demand for their computer client business.

A More Precise Foundary Business

Intel's foundry business has for quite some time underperformed; the business reported operating losses of $2.3 billion and $5.8 billion in 4Q24 and 3Q24, respectively, as you can see in the chart below. Investors can expect Tan to get these numbers under control in 2025, as the excessive spending under former CEO Pat Gelsinger is something of the past. Although Intel buys its semiconductors from TSMC, they are still one of the only U.S.-based foundries businesses in the U.S., and among the only ones when it comes to advanced chips. President Trump's promise to bring manufacturing back to the U.S. should favor Intel. In fact, we may even see Intel’s foundry business as its own wholly owned subsidiary sometime soon, as suggested by the VP of investor relations John Pitzer.

Valuation

Intel stock, for me, is grossly undervalued. The stock is down 44% over the last year, making it very attractive to investors. If we look across the industry, Intel's forward EV-to-sales ratio is far lower than industry competitors like AMD, Qualcomm (QCOM), and TSMC. The same trend follows in the forward price-to-sales ratio.

The graph I made below to compare ratios highlights Intel’s extremely low valuation. The market cap of Intel is just under $89 billion; they’re sitting just above their 52-week low of $17.66. The stock right now is undervalued, but investors shouldn’t view Intel as a growth stock but rather a value pick. I expect Intel to recover to stock prices of the low $30s after earnings at the end of the month.

What's Next?

Li-Bu Tan is on an efficacy run, and he doesn’t seem afraid of committing to big changes at Intel after dumping half of Altera. He definitely wants to line up with a more streamlined core business and give Intel a direct track to follow in 2025. I’m watching to see what happens with Intel’s foundry business when it comes to the joint venture with TSMC which was discussed earlier this month but got overshadowed by all the tariff panic. I also think we’ll see Tan announce further job cuts in 2025, something that might come up, in my opinion, on the company’s earnings call later this month.

I expect much healthier guidance for 2Q25, as they now have a decent amount of capital freed up to experiment with more cost-saving tactics and head into what should be a growth period for PCs. I remain buy-rated on Intel and look for a sub $20 per share entry position into the stock. I last wrote on Intel when it was trading at $22.38, and now 9% lower, I think the stock is attractively trading at a discount.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Trade War And Inflation Make Quick Service Fast Food Industry More Enticing
data.description:
With the ongoing trade war likely to push inflation ever higher, Americans, among others, will likely begin tightening their belts, which will put pressure on many consumer discretionary companies. However, I believe that quick service fast food restaurants, such as McDonald's (MCD) and Wendy's (WEN), actually stand to benefit as consumers continue to dine out while also looking to reduce spending on food outside of the home. In other words, they'll be looking for cheaper options. Meanwhile, pricier restaurants, including higher-end fast casual restaurants like Chipotle (CMG), will have to contend with downward pressure and more price-conscious consumers.

My thesis essentially breaks down to:

  • High inflation and economic uncertainty will encourage consumers to tighten their belts. Value-oriented industries, like fast food, may actually benefit from price-conscious consumers. Quite simply, fast food offers one of the cheaper and most convenient ways to get calories.
  • The United States economy is still in solid shape, and being less dependent on trade than many other countries, should have an easier time coping with near-term headwinds created by the trade war. That said, if the trade war worsens or continues for a prolonged period, it could drag the American economy down, potentially into a contraction. Likewise, economic contractions in other major economies will put pressure on the United States.
  • Regardless, fast food restaurants fared quite well during the Great Recession and may be a safe haven. The past doesn't guarantee the future, but fast food may prove more resilient (once again due to low prices) than other industries even if the economy should start to contract.
  • Most fast food ingredients are sourced locally, or at least nationally, which will, to some extent, shield quick service restaurants from the inflationary pressures of the trade war. Of course, even nationally sourced ingredients will be impacted by the trade war, and if prices for commodities like beef rise dramatically globally, it'll put upward pressure on domestically sourced ingredients.

At the end of this analysis, I will briefly examine a few quick service fast food stocks. These brief overviews are simply first impressions, but I do plan to take a deeper dive into individual quick service fast food stocks in the near future.

Regarding "safe havens," I must note that I am primarily talking about relatively. Any severe contractions or prolonged slowdowns will have a detrimental impact on essentially all companies. Even then, as far as market conditions are concerned, I am confident that the fast food sector will prove to be among the more resilient. However, many fast food companies are carrying high debt burdens, making their financial footing more tenuous.

More Affordable Prices Will Make Quick Service Fast Food a More Appetizing Choice

The ongoing trade war is likely to kick inflation into high gear, although the 90-day pause on "reciprocal" tariffs should ease inflationary pressures. However, with China still being hit with 145% tariffs, expect prices for many goods to rise nonetheless. Further, the 10% general tariff on most countries will push prices up, and even if all tariffs were dropped, inflation could continue apace.

The basic relationship between price and demand is well-known: Lower prices generally equate to increased demand, while higher prices dampen demand. With tariffs now in effect, we can expect the prices for many products to rise substantially, which should cool demand for many goods. Yet not all products and services will see prices increase at the same pace.

Admittedly, quick service fast food prices have risen in recent years, expanding by 60% between 2014 and 2024. Prices will likely rise in the future, but I believe the increase will not be as dramatic as it will be for other products, like automobiles and electronics. Demand for cars and electronics, among other things, will likely cool off. Yet, consumers will still consume and have money to spend. If fast food prices don't rise as quickly as prices for other goods, demand could shift to fast food, thus spurring sales.

Further, higher-priced items at fast food restaurants, like a Big Mac, could take an especially hard hit if inflation kicks up or the economy contracts. Value meals, like McDonald's $5 Meal Deal or Wendy's Biggie Bag should still offer enough value to attract customers. (This isn't to say that value meals are immune to price increases, but quick service restaurants will still offer as much value as they can.)

The shift from higher-priced and higher-margin items to value menu items will negatively impact fast food restaurants, but continued high overall demand will soften the blow.

Americans Love Spending on Food and Especially Take Out

In 2023, the average American consumer unit spent $9,985 on food, with $6,053 spent on food at home and $3,933 spent on food away from home. This is up from $5,259 and $3,030, respectively, in 2021. Spending on food away from home jumped 20% in 21-22 and 8.1% in 22-23.

One study found that the average American eats out three times a month and orders delivery or takeout about 4.5 times a month. Another survey found that the average American eats out 5 to 6 times a week.

Food Spending Habits During the Great Recession

You'll find tons of studies measuring spending habits during the Great Recession, and to no surprise, consumers tightened their belts, and spending on food, in particular, decreased, although calories consumed did not decline as steeply. Thus, people continued eating quite a bit, but strove to cut spending. Ultimately, between 2006 and 2009, consumers reduced food spending by 5%, according to data from the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS).

Consumers have become very price conscious. One way consumers tightened their belts was to spend more time hunting for deals and using coupons. In a sense, many people laid off, forced into lower-paying jobs, or who saw their hours cut, shifted from paid work to non-paid work (bargain hunting).

As seen in the chart above, food-away-from-home took the biggest hit. This includes fast food and more formal sit-down restaurants. Still, as I'll outline below, data suggests that while consumers ate out less often overall during the Great Recession, spending on fast food remained high, and in some cases, quick service chains enjoyed rising net income and revenues.

Fast Food Did Well During the Great Recession

I've seen articles in recent weeks arguing that an economic downturn would batter fast food as customers tighten their belts. Yet, that premise didn't strike me as immediately true for a few reasons. For one, fast food actually offers one of the cheaper ways to get calories.

The massive buying power of a company like McDonald's or Wendy's, in combination with easy-to-cook food and the ability to automate various aspects of operations, such as taking orders, allows fast food companies to offer affordable menu items. Eating food at home will typically be cheaper than fast food, but fast food remains an affordable option, especially when you factor in the time it takes to shop and cook.

So even when consumers batten down the financial hatches, that doesn't automatically mean quick service is off the menu. At least, I theorize this could be true. After all, you can get a $5 Biggie Bag from Wendy's or a $5 Meal Deal from McDonald's. Meanwhile, even the cheapest frozen pizzas in the grocery store often cost more than $5. By the time you're done buying ingredients to make a deli sandwich, let alone a burger, you're going to be spending a few bucks even with the cheapest stuff you grab on sale.

Data supports my argument that fast food did well during the Great Recession. Below you'll see that Yum! (YUM) enjoyed steady annual net income growth even amid the Great Recession. McDonald's took a short-term hit but quickly recovered. Wendy's only managed to maintain, but amid a major recession when other companies were dealing with slumping demand, treading water is still a good achievement.

[object HTMLElement]

Revenues for YUM, WEN, and MCD paint a similar picture:

[object HTMLElement]

Yet while these three fast food companies performed well in terms of revenues and net income, stock prices did take a hit. YUM's upward march was slowed, while MCD flatlined then declined. WEN suffered a steady drop. This was quite likely fueled by overall market sentiments and general cynicism surrounding the economy. If anything, the declining share prices for WEN, MCD, and YUM while underlying fundamentals (e.g. net income) remained solid-to-strong made them a more attractive buy. The same could prove true in a future recession, as well as during the trade war.

[object HTMLElement]

Fast Food is Global but America Remains King

For YUM, MCD, and WEN, the American market remains the key market. MCD and YUM both have a large presence outside of the United States. McDonald's has about 15,000 stores in the United States, along with 5,000 in Japan, 2,200 in France, 1,436 in the UK, and 1,500 in Germany. Even tiny Singapore has a whopping 151 locations. WEN isn't as exposed to international markets, but the company does plan to expand its global footprint, eying new markets as a key opportunity for growth.

Tariffs Could Actually Benefit Value-Oriented Fast Food Chains

For American quick service fast food restaurants, the American market is crucial. If American consumers come under pressure owing to inflation, and particularly inflation for products like electronics, spending habits may shift away from pricey foreign goods to local products and services, including fast food. A family might opt against upgrading their TV for now, but that could provide more money to spend at McDonald's, Disney World, local movie theaters, and other localized businesses.

Fast food restaurants source most of their ingredients on a more localized level. In the United States, McDonald's gets their beef primarily from Lopez Foods in Oklahoma City and Keystone Foods in Pennsylvania. Wendy's "fresh never frozen" mantra, meanwhile, makes sourcing locally especially important. In overseas markets too, quick service restaurants often get ingredients locally, so even if other countries start to raise tariffs, fast food giants may be at least partially insulated.

That said, tariffs could still have an impact. For example, the United States gets much of its potash, which is crucial for growing food and feed crops, from Canada. Potash is subjected to 10% (for non-CUSMA exempted). Potash prices have been on the rise already, and tariffs could push the price tag even higher.

Potash is only one of many examples of how tariffs could push up costs for fast food restaurants. Still, compared to automobile manufacturing and various other industries, the food industry remains more localized and less vulnerable.

Prices for beef, chicken, and other foodstuffs have been on the rise. Not only could tariffs affect even a relatively localized agriculture industry, but deporting immigrants could leave meat-processing factories and agricultural operations short-staffed. Wages could rise substantially, which is great for the workers, but will also contribute to higher prices for society as a whole.

A Quick Peek at Quick Service Fast Food Stocks and Quant Ratings

I've only just begun my analysis on individual fast food companies and am quite far from drawing any concrete conclusions. However, I do have some initial impressions to share.

McDonald's

Seeking Alpha's quant tools currently give MCD an F rating in terms of value. MCD scores poorly in Price / Sales (TTM) at $8.76 and the P/E GAAP (TTM) leaves a lot to desire at ~27. YOY revenue growth was 1.67%, below the 2.4% sector median, and growth is a major concern for me. McDonald's is already so mature of a company, with so many locations not just in the United States but also globally, that finding new areas to grow could prove especially challenging.

The quant ratings also give MCD a C rating for YOY revenue growth, which I'd say is rather generous. At a quick glance, I'd put MCD as a hold. Seeking Alpha's quant ratings agree.

Wendy's

Wendy's does a bit better, with the Seeking Alpha quant tools giving the company a solid B for valuation. The P/E GAAP (TTM) weighs in at an enticing ~14.00, below the 16.68 sector median. WEN gets a D- for growth, with their working capital shrinking by 25.72% and revenue growth (YoY) amounting to just 2.98%. It should be noted, however, that Wendy's is preparing to make a strong international push, which will generate risks but could also pave the way for higher growth.

The quant scores give WEN a B in valuation, although I admit that with Wendy's high debt levels, I do think that is rather generous. WEN's growth rating is a D-, in large part due to a dividend cut. However, Wendy's move to rein in the dividend I believe will improve the company's financial situation. Seeking Alpha's quant ratings give WEN a hold, which I believe is fair, but at a quick glance, I think there's a fair chance Wendy's will end up being a "buy" for me.

Yum!

Revenue YOY is up 6.68%, which is certainly encouraging, but the P/E GAAP (TTM) is quite high at ~28. Taco Bell is one of the crown jewels in Yum!'s portfolio and has been performing particularly well. Price/Sales (TTM) is also rather high at ~5.5. YUM is a hold according to the quant tools, but may end up a buy for me.

Considering Fast Casual Restaurants

I believe fast casual restaurants may feel the pressure of tightening consumer wallets, and thus I'm a bit wary of investing in them, at least for now. Further research could change my mind. The best fast casual restaurant chains have put in strong performances in recent years, and some of them have the potential for a lot of growth. Still, some chains are facing headwinds that make me especially nervous amid the trade war.

Chipotle's TTM P/E of ~41 would be easy to stomach if the brand was still rapidly expanding. Revenue (YOY, fiscal year ending 12/31/24) growth has been strong but may be leveling off, coming in at a respectable but not inspiring 13%, reaching $11.31 billion, and Chipotle already has $4.54 billion in debt. The risk of rising ingredient costs and already somewhat narrow profit margin at 13.56% could create pressure. Net income growth has been slow but steady in recent years. I wouldn't rule out investing in Chipotle, but with the potential for industry headwinds to kick up, I'm not rushing to invest in them either.

[object HTMLElement]

[object HTMLElement]

One fast casual stock that looks rather appealing at first glance is Wingstop (NYSE: WING), which in the 2024 fiscal year enjoyed YOY annual revenue growth of over 27%, along with a profit margin of 17.37% and an operating margin of 25.82%. Debit is a bit high at $1.27 billion, with revenues coming in at $625.81 million. The company has $315.91 million in cash. All in all, the high profit and operating margin suggest that Wingstop has a bit of room to cut prices if needed, and enough cash to sustain a crunch, although perhaps not a prolonged contraction. The sharp decline in WING's price does make it an interesting prospect.

Quick Take-Away: Quick Service Fast Food is an Industry to Watch

I haven't narrowed down which stocks, if any, to invest in, but I do believe that the quick service fast food industry offers some of the most appetizing prospects. I find the performance of McDonald's, Yum!, and Wendy's during the Great Recession to be especially promising, and I believe that even if a recession were to strike or the trade war drags on and slows growth, quick service restaurants can still do well.

data.title: Buy The Dips, Sell The Rips
data.description:
An investor who left to go left to go fishing on Saturday, April 5th and was completely disconnected from the rest of the world before being able to check their portfolios the following Saturday, probably would been quite happy with last week's performance from equities. The S&P rose almost six percent on the week while the NASDAQ climbed 7.3%.

Even the stodgy old Dow Jones Industrial Average rallied nearly five percent last week. Of course, what that fisherman would have missed was last week's daily stock gyrations that could easily give the most seasoned market veteran a serious case of acid reflux.

New tariff policies have triggered a large reset in the markets over the past 4-6 weeks. Even with the gains of last week and the rise in equities Monday, the S&P 500 is still down roughly eight percent for the year. The NASDAQ is off just over ten percent for 2025. Of course, several of major indexes briefly went into official 'bear market' territory before the recent rebound.

While the administration's new direction on tariffs will garner the justifiable blame for the recent market decline, the underlying reason for why markets have pulled back broadly is they were very overvalued coming into 2025. Equities based on many metrics (Price to sales ratio, Market Cap to GDP ratio, etc..) were trading significantly above where they were at the end of the Internet Boom in late 1999. The Internet Bust that followed lopped just over 40% off the S&P 500 peak to trough before that bear market finally bottomed in the summer of 2002. The NASDAQ fell over 80%.

I don't expect that kind of market rout this time around it should be noted. However, I have been consistently warning about a market selloff based on overvaluation for most of the past year. Here is a representative article on that topic ('This Doesn't End Well') from June of last year. The S&P 500 had two back-to-back gains of north of 20% in 2023 and 2024. This had not happened since the 1990s. 2025 was always looking at best a 'consolidation' year after all those gains and equities were highly vulnerable to a significant pull back as the year commenced.

So, where are valuation metrics after the recent market pullback? The Shiller PE ratio is at the higher end of its range. The only times it has been higher since the Internet Bust was late in 2021, just before 2022's bear market and just before this most recent pullback.

On a price to sales ratio basis, the S&P 500 is still 30% above its levels at the end of the Internet Boom it should also be noted. What about on a more traditional P/E basis? Citigroup just cut their year-end S&P 500 target from 6,500 to 5,800. They also took down their S&P 500 earnings estimate from $270 to $255 for FY2025. I would expect other investment banks to follow as tariffs will cut profit margins for numerous industries.

Using Citigroup's new earnings target for the S&P 500, that leaves the index trading at 21.3 forward earnings. Not exactly bargain territory given all the economic uncertainty and compared to historical valuations. Especially taken into consideration, the S&P 500's paltry 1.3% dividend yield compared with the 'risk-free' yield from the 10-Year Treasury of 4.4%.

Currently, my portfolio is currently allocated approximately 30% to short-term treasuries. The rest is largely in covered call holdings around the few stocks that I am finding have reasonable values in what has been for the last year an overbought market. Lately, I have been focusing on companies with solid balance sheets that should have minimal impacts from the emerging trade wars. Acadia Pharmaceuticals (ACAD), a biopharma concern I last profiled in March would one example of that sort of company/stock. Dynavax Technologies (DVAX) which I also highlighted last month, would be another.

Right now, I am mainly buying the dips and selling the rips. This means when equities rally like they did at points last week and also on Monday, I 'roll' the options around some of my covered call positions whose option expirations are coming up within the next few months and don't look like they will expire in the money. I rolled eight of these positions yesterday as the market continued its rally from late last week.

When stocks plunge, I deploy some of my 'dry powder' into the market via covered call orders. This will remain my MO until we see some stabilization in the markets. What will that likely consist of? First, I think we need some new trade deals to be announced to show the new tariff policies are bearing some fruit. I also think we need to see the crazy swings in the 10-Year Treasury we have seen in April to cease.

The yield on the 10-Year Treasury had a huge move in recent trading sessions from just under four percent to close at 4.5% last Friday. Despite the huge move up in rates last week, it should be noted the 10-Year Treasury was near 4.8% in early January before the new administration was inaugurated. It would be reassuring if this yield moved back closer to the four percent threshold before its recent spike higher.

Finally, I would like to see the VIX to come back under the 20 level it traded at before the huge spike up in volatility triggered by announcements of tariffs. It will probably be weeks if not months before all three of those criteria are satisfied. Until then, I plan to be very nimble with my portfolio allocation as described previously. In short, buy the dips and sells the rips.

data.title: SCHD Will Continue To Be A Bitter Disappointment... Unless The World Falls Apart
data.description:
Those who follow my work closely likely know by now that I am not a fan of the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHD). At the end of the day, I believe that the best investing approach for the vast majority of investors is to capture the best returns. I also understand the appeal of high dividend yielding opportunities. And that is something that has historically drawn investors to SCHD. However, even if that is your objective over capturing a stronger total return, there are better ways than by buying SCHD.

In my last article about the ETF, I made clear that there have been periods of time when returns for the ETF have been comparable with the broader market. I have also espoused in the past the view that there could be windows of time that we could cherry-pick when it might outperform the major indices. But over the long run, I view this as a suboptimal opportunity, both for those focused on achieving a strong total return, and for those focused on capturing an attractive yield. Despite the extreme market volatility that we have seen as of late, and the fact that there has been a short window of time during which SCHD has outperformed the broader market, I maintain my bearish assessment of the ETF.

Still a bad play

The last article that I wrote about SCHD was published in January of this year. To be precise, it was published on January 18th. From that time through today, the market has experienced tremendous volatility. As of the end of the day on April 11th, the S&P 500 (SP500), as an example, is down 10.27%. This is a hefty decline caused by the US entering into a trade war with the world. And the S&P 500 has not been the only casualty of this. The Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) has fared far better, but it is still down 7.14%. Meanwhile, the NASDAQ (COMP:IND) is down a very painful 14.65%.

Those who are fans of SCHD tout its attractive yield and the stability of the underlying companies as a reason to own it. The way they view it, these two attributes should allow it to be a better place to store your capital when times get tough. I have admitted in the past that this is true. But the only opinion on the matter is that these benefits are not worth the downside of subpar returns. To put this in perspective, during the same timeframe that we are talking about, SCHD is down only 8.80%. This is better than both the S&P 500 and the NASDAQ. But it does trail the Dow Jones Industrial Average. These returns do factor in distributions received. So during this window of time, SCHD would have only been superior, marginally, to the S&P 500. It would have been significantly better than owning the NASDAQ. But it would have fallen short of the Dow Jones Industrial Average by a small extent.

If you are expecting to be able to time downside in the market, SCHD can certainly be a valid opportunity. But when you look at it over basically any long period of time, it becomes clear that even being able to use this as a market timing tool results in suboptimal returns. Before my January article, the last article that I had written about SCHD was published on June 3rd of 2024. As has always been the case, I labeled the ETF a ‘sell’ candidate. From that time through today, SCHD has achieved total upside of only 0.88%. That does place it above the 0.02% loss achieved by the NASDAQ. But as the chart above illustrates, it places it behind both the S&P 500 and the Dow Jones Industrial Average.

We can see this once again when looking at the ETF and how it has performed since my original ‘sell’ rating on September 15th of 2023. This time, the picture looks extreme. From that time through the time of this writing, SCHD has achieved a return for investors of 9.98%. That pales in comparison to the other market indices. The Dow Jones Industrial Average has risen by nearly double that, with a return of 19.53%. The S&P 500 and the NASDAQ have captured nearly identical upside of 23.20% and 23.40%, respectively. Unless you only have a couple of years left before you need your capital, and unless you expect a major market decline, I don't see any reason why SCHD would make sense for a portfolio.

Even those who are bullish about SCHD will point out that achieving total return is not the only reason for owning it. Relying on the attractive yield provided by it, such as using it as income, can be another drive to purchase units of it. But even this argument results in the conclusion that this is a suboptimal opportunity. The yield of 3.49% that it has at this time is really not that great, especially with where interest rates are at this time. Furthermore, in my previous articles about it, I created an alternative portfolio of five large companies that have attractive yields that have, on the whole, meaningfully outperformed SCHD.

The reason why I picked these particular companies is because they are operationally diverse, they generate attractive yields, they don’t include more exotic investment opportunities like REITs and MLPs, and because they are large companies. These are all attributes that the holdings of SCHD have, though one difference is that the holdings of SCHD are supposed to match the Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 Index (DJUSDIV), which is known for having securities in it that have a track record of consistently paying out distributions. The five holdings that I chose, shown below, do fall short of this last measure. These holdings happen to be AT&T (T), Truist Financial Corporation (TFC), Dominion Energy (D), Walgreens Boots Alliance (WBA), and Citizens Financial Group (CFG).

Even with that being the case, their returns have been demonstrably better than what SCHD has seen most of the time. In the aforementioned table, you can see their individual performance and their aggregate performance if an investor had put the same amount of money into each one back in September 2023 when I first wrote about SCHD. From January 18th through the time of this writing, they would have underperformed, achieving a downside of 10.09%. This number is 8.96% to the negative if we exclude the problem child that is Walgreens Boots Alliance. But even so, these figures aren't all that bad considering the broader market. And if we look back from the original time that I wrote about SCHD, we would have an upside of 25.39%, which would be about 2.5 times greater than what SCHD achieved. Excluding, once again, Walgreens Boots Alliance, we would have an upside of 43.56%.

If your objective is to capture attractive yield, buying these five stocks would have given you a better outcome as well. Even if we factor in the fact that Walgreens Boots Alliance suspended its quarterly dividend on January 30th of this year, the average yield of these five businesses stands at 4.00%. That's slightly better than the 3.49% achieved by SCHD. And if we exclude the problem child again, the yield grows to 5.01%. That is still dividing the distribution across all five holdings.

Before I end this article, one other thing I would like to touch on is the overall composition of SCHD as it stands today. Because of how things have changed in the market, the ETF has actually changed a bit. In the table above, you can see the different sectors that it is invested in. The table also shows how this composition has changed over time. Most notably, financials continue to represent a larger and growing portion of the ETF. They have grown from 14.54% in June 2023 to 18.73% at the end of last year (which is the most recent time for which data is available). The most recent reading is up from the 18.20% reported for September of last year. Healthcare has recently increased to 16.67%. We have also seen an increase in the consumer staples category. Other aspects have declined, such as consumer discretionary and materials. The ETF is still incredibly diversified. But those who are going to be long it should know that, depending on how the broader economy does, these changes in composition could have an impact on its overall performance. During times of crisis, for instance, I would expect financials, the consumer discretionary category, and the energy space, to all experience disproportionate levels of weakness. The materials space could also be negatively affected. By comparison, healthcare, consumer staples, and communication services would likely hold up better.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I remain perplexed why SCHD has so many adherents. In a fairly short window of time since I last wrote about it, it has admittedly performed better than two of the three major market indices. But the outperformance relative to the S&P 500 is not tremendous by any means. Over any long window of time, you can expect SCHD to drastically underperform the broader market. Those who are looking to retire or otherwise cash out of their portfolios in the next couple of years might be able to justify owning SCHD. But if you don't fit in that category, buying it should only result in disappointment.

data.title: StoneX Group: A Hidden Gem, Creating Lots Of Financial Value
data.description:
Shares of StoneX Group Inc. (NASDAQ:SNEX) have been a very strong outperformer in recent years, as the connecting link to markets has been performing well on an organic basis, accompanied by real dealmaking efforts.

While the outperformance is to be applauded, shares trade at very reasonable levels, which I attribute some kind of opaqueness around the business. I like the performance of the business a great deal, yet fail to have conviction to hold a position in size, in this impressive financial value creator.

Connecting Clients To Markets

The paragraph header is basically what StoneX is about. The company provides institutional-grade global market access, clearing and execution through a single trusted partner.

The company monetizes client activity over this network through commission and spreads on trades, interest earned on deposits and fees charges for access, expertise and intelligence.

The scale of its offerings is huge, including over 40 exchanges, nearly 20,000 OTC products provided, over 50,000 of paying clients, billions in assets held by clients, and trillions in volume traded.

The company reports its results across fourth segments, generating over $1.8 billion in very profitable net operating revenues. The largest segment is a $742 million commercial platform which performs risk mitigation solutions, this being a very profitable segment with margins reported in the mid-fifties.

The second-largest segment is a $656 million institutional business which includes a global trading, execution and clearing platform, with margins reported in the low-forties. These are complemented by a $314 million self-direct/retail business and $197 million payments/treasury management platform.

The company has been combining organic growth, with some 35 deals over the past decade. This has resulted in huge capital gains delivered upon for investors in the long run. A mere $20 stock in 2019 traded in the low fifties in spring of 2024, and now has essentially doubled again to $78 per share.

The Valuation

In November of last year, the company reported its results for the fiscal year 2024. Net operating revenues rose by 7% to $1.77 billion as the company grew operating profits by 10% to $354 million, with net earnings up 9% to $261 million.

Amidst modest dilution incurred, to the tune of 2%, the company grew earnings by 7% to $7.96 per share. Given this earnings power, and net asset value per share reported at $53 per share, shares trading in the $60s at the time looked very reasonably priced.

Investors Get Excited

In February, StoneX reported a 17% increase in first quarter net operating revenues to $492 million. Quarterly earnings rose by 23% to $85 million, equal to $2.54 per share, as the share tally rose to 32 million and change. In the meantime, the net asset value per share rose to $55 and change.

After some smaller deals, the company reached and announced a $900 million deal to acquire R.J. O´Brien, the U.S.' oldest futures brokerage. The price tag is the equity portion of the business, as assumed debt of $143 million, makes for a price tag of $1.04 billion, with the total deal tag running at around $1.1 billion if fees are included.

This compares to a near $2.4 billion equity valuation based on 32 million shares trading at $75 ahead of the deal announcement, as shares rose some $3 per share, or about $100 million in response to the deal, as investors give their vote of confidence. With a $766 million revenue contribution and $170 million EBITDA contribution, the business is valued at around 1.5 times sales and about 6 times EBITDA, both very reasonable multiples.

These multiples look quite modest, but financial statements, both profit- and loss statement and account balances, are a bit hard to read into. This is in part because the nature of the activities result in a huge gross to net ratio, both in terms of revenues but balance sheet items as well.

Part of the deal will be paid for in shares in fact, $275 million worth, thereby allowing to keep the leverage increase in check. Investors are likely upbeat on these multiples as well as the fact that $50 million in synergies are seen down the road, that number even excluding potential revenue synergies.

What Now?

The reality is that StoneX seems a bit of an opaque business. However, amidst savvy M&A deals, which involves dilution, but overall delivers on solid growth on a per-share basis, SNEX shares looked quite cheap. Despite the great momentum over the past years, shares trade very modest earnings multiple and a modest premium to book.

With earnings power reported at $8 per share in 2024, the business has started 2025 on a strong foot, and the latest and substantial deal looks quite accretive as well. Investors, however, have to look forward to profitable growth to see returns, in the absence of net buybacks and dividends, but the operating performance in recent times has been quite impressive, I must say. Moreover, with earnings trending around $10 per share, shares still look incredibly cheap based on the earnings power of the business.

Nonetheless, while the growth, M&A track record and valuations look highly compelling, even after shares have quadrupled over the past five years, I have some reservations. This might have to do with the opaqueness of the business, the fact that many revenues are tied to trade in physical (commodity) contracts and the Middle East in particular.

However, the business has been performing in an impressive manner. This means that a small position might be warranted, likely to keep a signaling function, although that I fail to have huge conviction to hold a huge stake in the business here.

data.title: Why Goldman Sachs Stock Is Flat Despite Solid Early Trends In Earnings Growth
data.description:
In its first quarter (Q1) earnings, The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (NYSE:GS) - a global investment bank also deemed a “Global Systemically Important Bank” (G-SIB) - continues to retain its standing as the leader of the financial services sector so far this earnings season.

Overall line item trends indicate early indicators of outperformance relative to the previous Fiscal Year (FY) which, in itself, was a solid outperformer itself:

If trends are to continue, net earnings will be 36% higher than the previous FY's despite a comparatively modest 12% uptick in revenue along with an 8% increase in operating expenses.

When considering line item performance relative to revenue, a trend that's evident is that operating expenses relative to revenue have actually been falling since FY 2023.

As a result, net earnings (and profit margin) has been rising to the percentage-share highs seen in FY 2021. In fact, if trends were to continue, parity with the highs could possibly be attained by the end of FY 2025.

A persistent trend that was highlighted in the previous article about the bank's full year results persists in this quarter: deal-making (once a cornerstone of the bank's prowess in the industry) has shrunk away leading to market-facing segments such as Equities and FICC (“Fixed Income, Currency & Commodities”) as well as client wealth management accounting for 75% of the total revenue.

Quick Note: “net revenue” or “revenue” is used in place of what banks refer to as “Revenue Before Provision for Loan Losses.” With more market-related activities, it is likely that this provisioning becomes even more pertinent.

Moreover, provision for credit losses went from $351 million in Q4 2024 to $287 million in Q1 2025, suggesting an overall improvement in business operations.

However, in a statement released on the same day as the earnings release, CEO David Solomon stated that "we are entering the second quarter with a markedly different operating environment than earlier this year" - a warning that Q2 onwards might need a certain grounding of expectations.

Recession Worries Deepen

CEO Solomon wasn't the only major financial services leader to essentially issue a warning about the impact of the ongoing tariff war. JPMorgan (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon stated that he believes its more likely than not that were will be a recession in the present year while BlackRock (BLK) CEO Larry Fink warned that the impact of the tariff war could be widespread.

Note: The tariff war - and the likely drivers necessitating it - was covered in detail in an earlier article.

As a result of market uncertainty over the impact of tariffs on consumption, inflation and the markets, long-term borrowing rates have dropped, making it harder for banks to realize sizeable profits on loans. In addition, deal-making divisions are expected to take a hit as mergers & acquisitions (M&A) and IPO plans are shelved.

As it stands, a drop in deal-making isn't a massive jolt given its reduced role but it is still a net contributor to the bottom line. The ongoing tussle between China and the U.S. is steadily growing deeper, with the government of the former now directing its airlines to not take deliveries of Boeing jets and associated aircraft parts from U.S. suppliers. This marks another part of Goldman Sachs' vulnerability: about a steady 11% of its revenue has been originating from Asia for a few years now - with a substantial portion likely with some connection to China, either directly or indirectly. If the Chinese government were to go on to order restrictions on relationships with American financial institutions, there is a possibility that this will be yet another blow on revenue growth.

In fact, CEO Solomon had explicitly stated that President Trump's policies "reset the prospect of forward growth pretty significantly all over the world."

In Conclusion

As markets tumble, the prospect of a rate cut to spur lending activities is inevitable. While lower rates means lower profit booking, the spurring of economic activity might net banks greater revenue from a wider base of clients - which is why financials have now tended to perk up in conviction during periods of stronger recessionary outlook.

Despite CEO Solomon's warnings about the forward outlook, the stock did rise by a little under 2% in the course of the trading session on the 14th of April. While after-hours trading and pre-market trading session hinted at an erasure of these gains as a result of intensifying trade disputes, it's more likely than not that the stock prices of the likes of Goldman Sachs would far less badly than most other sectors.

Given that the bank also issues dividends (and had, in fact, authorized a respectable $3 quarterly dividend), there is some strength to the argument that holding a position in the bank's stock would do well for portfolio diversification in the year to come.

data.title: SkyWest: The Best Airline Stock Heading Into Earnings
data.description:
In November, I covered SkyWest, Inc. (NASDAQ:SKYW) with a strong buy rating. The stock price performance, however, has not been satisfactory with a 15% decline, underperforming the S&P 500 (SP500), which lost 5.6%. The stock price is still up 80% from my buy call in November 2023 compared to a 18.9% return for the S&P 500. So, the stock price decline since November is not dramatic.

In this report, I will discuss what I like about SkyWest and I will discuss the company’s upcoming earnings. Additionally, I will update my stock price target for the company.

SkyWest Business Is Shielded Very Well

Airlines are generally exposed to macroeconomic events that affect costs as well as unit revenues. That is also why we recently saw Delta (DAL) and Frontier Airlines (ULCC) pull their full year guidance.

SkyWest, however, is to a major extent isolated from these fluctuations. The company operates under capacity agreements which include minimum guarantees. So, whether base fares are high or low there is no revenue impact on the revenues. Even in times of economic weakening, the business is shielded through minimum guarantees set out in capacity agreements with Delta, United (UAL), American Airlines (AAL) and Alaska Airlines (ALK).

Similarly, fuel costs can be passed through to the parties for which SkyWest operates the capacity agreements.

The business, however, is not fully isolated from market dynamics. Prorate agreements and charter flights represent 13% of revenues. The prorate agreements allow SkyWest to share in the base fares and expose the regional airline to virtually all costs. Similarly, on charter flights the company is also exposed to the market dynamics.

The reason why SkyWest stock may have sold off recently is because there could be a fear that the mainline carriers will require less regional capacity flying if market conditions soften further and it is probably also a sentiment issue. Usually, when stocks in a sector or industry decline or gain there is little regard for whether the dynamics of a specific subset of companies are materially different from the peer group and as a result potentially could offer a better risk profile.

What Are Analysts Expecting For The First Quarter Earnings?

For the first quarter (expected April 24th), analysts expect SkyWest to report revenues of $946.9 million indicating 17.8% year-on-year growth. That should translate into earnings per share of $2.05, which indicates 41% growth. The company expects block hour execution to be up 12% in 2025 providing support to the higher revenues in addition to increased unit rates.

The company has not guided for EPS, but it shared during the fourth quarter earnings call that its EPS could be around $9 per share for the full year. The first quarter tends to be the weakest quarter so a $2.05 earnings per share would be quite a solid start. The significant growth in EPS should really come from growing the top line while keeping costs growth low.

For the first quarter, over the past six months, the EPS and revenue revisions have been positive with revenue estimates improving 3.4% and EPS estimates revisions up 2.9%. Those are not huge changes, but they are positive.

The company has a good history on beating analyst revenue and EPS estimates. Over the past two years, the company beat EPS estimates each quarter and only produced one miss on revenues, which was in Q4 2023 when it missed estimates by 0.9%. In all other quarters revenue beats ranged between 1.3% and 4.9%.

During the first quarter call, I hope that SkyWest management will be able to reconfirm and highlight its shielding from weakening in air fares and provide some insights on how the current market turmoil is affecting demand for regional jet operations if there is any impact.

Strong Buy Rating Reiterated For SkyWest

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For SkyWest, I am maintaining my Strong Buy rating with a $126.98 price target providing 49% upside. I have lifted my price target by around $7 per share. The valuation is based on 2024 earnings in line with a peer group EV/EBITDA multiple. This means that we are not factoring in the premium that SkyWest usually trades at nor are we incorporating the upside driven by growth in the years ahead. Putting it very simple, at this very moment, the stock price should already be at $127.

EBITDA is set to grow at a rate of 20.6% annually through 2027. Free cash flow growth is expected to be8% annually but we do note that free cash flow in 2024 was around 25% weaker than expected and the estimate for 2025 has also come down by around 25%. So, the free cash flow is definitely a watch item.

Conclusion: SkyWest Shielded Business Is Underappreciated

I believe that SkyWest stock is currently significantly undervalued. The company has some exposure to fluctuations in base fares and fuel costs through its prorate agreements and charter flights. These accounted for around 13% of the revenues in 2024.

However, the company is generally shielded very well from fluctuations in revenue and cost dynamics through capacity agreements that also provide minimum guarantees. I believe that this is not reflected in the current stock price nor is the strong execution in 2024. As a result, I maintain my strong buy rating and increase my price target to $126.98.

data.title: Riverwater Partners Small-Cap Q1 2025 Letter
data.description:
Small-cap stocks took a step back in this quarter. This followed strong momentum after the November election. Tariff announcements created significant business uncertainty as they flipped between on again off again tariff announcements with two of our largest trading partners, Canada and Mexico. The current administration’s tariff policies differ in two ways from the last tariff policy initiations in 2018. First, the previous round of tariffs came after significant corporate tax cuts. Second, the last round was focused on China and not Canada, Mexico, and the rest of the world.

Canada and Mexico are intricately enmeshed in our supply chains. We have had the largest free trade zone in the world for over 30 years with NAFTA and then its successor, the USMCA. Businesses gained confidence to allocate capital in both Canada and Mexico thanks to these agreements. Business sentiment surveys are already reflecting sudden uncertainty around these trade relationships.

We own companies across our strategies that either sell outside the US or have supply chains outside the US. After completing a thorough review for every holding, we are comfortable with our current positioning and believe we have lower exposures than our benchmarks. However, it can be difficult to assess benchmark weights as there is no aggregate data available for exposures by country.

Government Spending and Fiscal Policy

Fiscal policy shifts are adding another layer of uncertainty to the economic outlook. The new administration’s emphasis on spending cuts coincides with the final phaseout of pandemic-era stimulus measures. This will likely slow economic growth in the short term.

President Trump’s recent decision to eliminate the penny is symbolic. It shows how cost-cutting policies can have hidden consequences. Like the penny—which costs 3.7¢ to mint but is worth only 1¢—many federal programs are being evaluated for their immediate cost rather than their broader economic impact. This mindset echoes the administration’s tariff approach: headline-driven policies that overlook supply chain complexity. For example, it’s estimated the average American-made car could cost $3,500 to $10,000 or more under the proposed tariffs.

Eliminating the penny may seem like a cost-saving measure, but it raises further questions. The nickel, which costs 13.8¢ to produce versus its 5¢ face value, results in an even greater loss—8.3¢ per coin. If more nickels are minted to offset the penny loss, the US Mint’s net losses could increase. And removing both coins would force retailers to round prices, potentially pushing them higher and contributing to inflation. Tariffs could have a similar effect: narrow, targeted measures that lead to broad and compounding economic consequences as trade partners retaliate. Tariff announcements are coming in as we write, and clearly the market has reacted negatively to both US tariffs and affected countries' counter tariffs.

As we look to add new companies to our portfolios, we are certainly cognizant of companies’ supply chain dependencies and foreign sales exposures. We’re likely to avoid businesses vulnerable to an extended tariff war. Fortunately, we believe small-caps are better positioned than large caps as it relates to tariff exposures because of their domestic orientation.

Valuation: The Small-cap Opportunity

Investors pulled back from the Russell 2000 in Q1 2025, driving a 9.5% decline and underperformance versus large caps, with the S&P 500 declining 4.3% over the same period. This reversal highlights small-cap volatility in response to policy shifts. While the media focuses obsessively on the “Magnificent Seven,” small-caps are quietly trading below their historical valuation averages. Using the Shiller CAPE (cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings) methodology, which smooths earnings over a ten-year period to temper the effects of earnings booms and busts, we find compelling evidence that small caps offer significant value. Thanks, as always, to our friends at Furey Research Partners for actually creating a CAPE for the small-cap market. Analysis of Furey’s work on the Russell 2000 CAPE ratio reveals:

  • Small-cap CAPE valuation is trading below its long-term average at 37.5x
  • Large-cap CAPE valuation is near one standard deviation above its long-term average
  • Small vs. large relative CAPE valuation is near all-time lows
  • Historical data suggests small-caps may deliver approximately 8.0% annualized returns over the next decade from current levels, compared to just 3.5% for the S&P 500

This significant valuation discount suggests that despite near-term challenges, the stage is being set for potential small-cap outperformance over the long term. The last time small caps traded at such a significant discount to large caps was in the late 1990s, which was followed by several years of substantial outperformance.

Looking Ahead: Navigating the Changing Landscape

Despite the challenging quarter, two potential catalysts could drive small-cap outperformance as 2025 progresses. First, a resolution of tariff and fiscal uncertainties. Second, valuation recognition: the substantial discount of small-cap valuations compared to large may finally attract increased investor attention, especially if large cap growth begins to slow. Valuations are never a catalyst in themselves, but they could certainly add fuel to the fire if they do take the mantle of leadership.

Conclusion

While small-caps face real challenges in the current environment, history suggests that periods of significant underperformance and valuation discounts often present the most attractive entry points for long-term investors. The Russell 2000’s current CAPE ratio – well below historical averages – provides a substantial margin of safety that should help mitigate downside risks while positioning for future appreciation.

For investors with appropriate time horizons, the current environment may offer an opportunity to increase small-cap exposure at valuations that have historically preceded periods of outperformance. While near-term volatility is likely to persist until tariff and fiscal policies stabilize, the compelling valuation case for small caps continues to strengthen.

Adam J. Peck, CFA, Founder and CIO

The information contained herein represents the opinion of Riverwater and should not be construed as personalized or individualized investment advice. Analysis and opinion expressed in this report are subject to change without notice. The securities identified and described do not represent all of the securities purchased, sold or recommended for client accounts. The reader should not assume that an investment in the securities identified was or will be profitable

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
